coredata.dc:title: Luminescence properties of Eu3+/CDs/PVA composite applied in light conversion film
coredata.dc:creator: He, Jiangling
He, Youling
Zhuang, Jianle
Zhang, Haoran
Lei, Bingfu
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, blue-light-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were composited with red-light-emitting europium ions (Eu3+) solutions under the synergistic reaction of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare the light conversion film. The formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film was detailedly discussed. It is the first report that this composite was synthesized through direct recombination of CDs and Eu3+ solutions instead of traditional methods based on Eu3+ coordination compound. Furthermore, tunable photoluminescence property can be successfully achieved by controlling the ratio of CDs to doped Eu3+, this property can meet the variable light component requirements for different species of plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Tunable photoluminescence
Eu3+/CDs/PVA
Film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties, optical analysis and a proposed formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (a) and TEM image (b) of CDs. Size distribution (c) of CDs, the error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
FTIR spectrum (a) and XPS spectra: survey scan of CDs (b), high-resolution C1s (c) and O1s (d) peaks and the fitting curves of CDs.

UV-Vis absorption spectrum (black), PL emission spectrum (blue) and PL excitation spectrum (red) of a dilute aqueous solution of the CDs (a). PL emission spectra of a dilute aqueous solution of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths (b). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XRD patterns (a) and light transmittance spectra (b) of pure PVA film and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film. The inset in b shows the photographs of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film under the day light.

PL emission spectra of the Eu3+/CDs/PVA film at different excitation wavelengths (a), PL emission spectra from 360 nm to 650 nm (b) and from 570 nm to 640 nm (c) of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film, CDs aqueous solution and Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution (ex = 360 nm). ATR-IR spectra of pure PVA film and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film (d), (e) and (f) were the high magnified ATR-IR spectra.
Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film.
PL emission spectra (a) and CIE 1931 chromaticity space (b) of different concentration of doped-Eu3+ in Eu3+/CDs/PVA film.

head.ce:title: Luminescence properties of Eu3+/CDs/PVA composite applied in light conversion film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agricultural, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guang Zhou, 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agricultural

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, blue-light-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were composited with red-light-emitting europium ions (Eu3+) solutions under the synergistic reaction of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare the light conversion film. The formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film was detailedly discussed. It is the first report that this composite was synthesized through direct recombination of CDs and Eu3+ solutions instead of traditional methods based on Eu3+ coordination compound. Furthermore, tunable photoluminescence property can be successfully achieved by controlling the ratio of CDs to doped Eu3+, this property can meet the variable light component requirements for different species of plants.

It is the first report that this composite was synthesized through direct recombination of CDs and Eu3+ solutions instead of traditional methods based on Eu3+ coordination compound. Tunable photoluminescence property can be successfully achieved by controlling the ratio of CDs to doped-Eu3+, this property can meet the variable light component requirements for different species of plants.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the problems of food security have aroused a hot discussion throughout the world [1,2]. In China, the food security is particularly serious. China has more than 20% of the world population and 8% of the total world arable land [3]. China's per capita arable land is less than 1.5 acres and the decreasing trend of this number are irreversible and the quality of the land is getting worse. The development of Chinese future agriculture must be faced with some serious situations, such as shortage of resource, ecological deterioration, the security of nearly 1.6 billion people, realization of the ecological environment and sustainable development. All of them need the vigorous development of modern agriculture science and technology. As one of significant parameters for plant growth, light is not only the energy source of plant photosynthesis, but also the power source of sprout, blossom, and fruit as well as other morphogenesis for plants [4]. The absorption of blue and red light by plants has been measured as 90% [5], which indicates that plant development and physiology is strongly influenced by blue or red light [6]. Blue and red light are important for plants photosynthesis. To synthesize blue and red light convert film through an easy and simple method is with great significance for the growth of plants, and then can bring a lot of benefits to human's life. Hence, the best spectra which artificial simulated for plant's photosynthesis can be regard as both fertilizer and pesticide, improve plant's growing conditions to help them grow better [7,8].

Photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) are a new form of zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials which have attracted wide attention due to the superiority in benign chemical composition, tunable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [9–11,37–42] and shown a tremendous potential as versatile nanomaterials for a wide range of applications such as photocatalysis, heavy metal ion detection, and biological labeling [12–14]. Many researchers have extracted CDs through sample method from various carbon sources [15–17]. It can be found that most of the CDs exhibits blue light with wide wavelenghts while excited by UV radiation [18,19]. The low cost, low cytotoxicity and wide region of blue-light-emitting make CDs become an excellent blue-light conversion agents for light conversion film.

Luminescence from the rare earth element (RE3+) originates from transitions between 4f orbitals, these transitions are forbidden on symmetry grounds, which are (4fn) well shielded by 5s2 and 5p [6] orbitals. Therefore, emission transitions yield sharp lines in the optical spectra. It has been known for a long time that europium ions (Eu3+) show strong red fluorescence in variety of lattices.

In this work, we prepared blue-light-emitting CDs. And then dispersing the CDs and EuCl3 into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix leading to the formation of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film by a simple and conventional solution cast technique for light conversion. In order to investigate the formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film, the XRD patterns, light transmittance spectra, PL spectra and infrared spectra between pure PVA film and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film were further measured. In addition, by adjusting the mixing proportion between CDs and doped-Eu3+ concentration, tunable photoluminescence was successfully achieved. Only a few researchers reported the composites based on CDs and traditional coordination compounds containing rare earth ions [20,36], This work stands out for one-step fabrication of Eu3+/CDs. Furthermore, Eu3+/CDs/PVA film exhibits outstanding light conversion property.

Ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid salt (EDTA-2Na), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and EuCl3 were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water was produced through a Millipore water purification system (≥18 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used throughout the whole experiments.

The preparation procedure is modified from previous report [21]. 1.5 g (EDTA-2Na) was placed in a quartz boat and was heated in a furnace tube under N2 atmosphere at 400 °C for 4 h. This resulted in the formation of black solid which was collected at room temperature. Then the black solid should be ground in agate mortar to form carbonaceous powder. The carbonaceous powder was dissolve in deionized water (The rate of solid and liquid is 1 g: 400 mL) under ultrasonic processing for 30 min. Then the solution was filtered with the 0.22 μm filtration membrane. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 30 min and the supernatant solution, which was brown and transparent, was collected, and then was subjected to dialysis with molecular weight cut off (MWCO: 3500), against water for one day in order to remove smaller particles and impurities.

1 mL (0.01 mg/mL) of as-prepared CDs aqueous solution and serial concentrations of EuCl3 solutions (1 mL) was mixed with 10 mL PVA solution (10 wt%) by slow shaking in order to obtain uniform Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution. Moreover, the mixture was coated onto glass or plastic substrate and then dried in an oven under 60 °C for 12 h to obtain Eu3+/CDs/PVA composite films.
The calculation of the quantum yield (QY).

The quantum yield was measured using quinine sulfate as the standard and calculated with the following equation:
(1)
Q =
Q R
I
I R
O
D R
O D
n 2
n R 2
where the “Q” is the quantum yield, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index, and the optical density (OD), which is measured on a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The subscript “R” refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield, quinine sulfate (QS) used in present work. The quinine sulfate (literature Q
R = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (n = 1.33) and the CDs were dissolved in deionized water (n = 1.33) [22].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a TECNAI 10 (PHILIPS) electron microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis was performed using SPM/AFM Dimension 3100. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a XD-2X/M4600 X-ray diffract meter (40 kV, 20 mA, λ = 1.54051 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected by employing an X-ray photoelectron Spectroscope (AXIS ULTRA DLD, Kratos). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Attenuated total reflection infrared (ATR-IR) spectra were measured by an Avatar 360 FT-IR (Thermo Nicolet) spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs and CDs/PVA hybrids were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV-Vis absorption spectrum was measured by UV/Vis spectrometer UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan). The quantum yield was measured according to the established procedure shown in Equation (1).

XRD patterns of the CDs are presented in Fig. 1 a. There is a broad peak around 24.4°, corresponding to 0.363 nm interlayer spacing of CDs. No sharp peaks are observed in the XRD patterns, confirming the amorphous character of the CDs. The size of the as-prepared CDs was investigated by TEM. Fig. 1b shows typical TEM image of CDs derived from EDTA-2Na. The as-prepared CDs are spherical in shape and highly mono-dispersed with relatively uniform. Moreover, the TEM micrograph depicts the nanoscale size of CDs with average particle diameter is 3 nm, and Fig. 1c gives the size distribution of CDs, which are with nanoscale range from 1 to 8 nm, and mainly are located at 3 nm. This result further conforms to the TEM image.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate the bonding composition of CDs and its functional groups, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The peak at 3400 cm−1 can be ascribed to the OH group. The peak at 2972 cm−1 is attributed to CH bonds. The peaks at 1651 cm−1 and 1382 cm−2 can be ascribed to CO groups and NO2 group, respectively. In addition, the band at 1053 cm−1 appears due to the CN bond. XPS measurements were performed for the surface elemental analysis of CDs derived from EDTA-2Na. As shown in Fig. 2b, three major peaks of C, O and N obviously emerged in the spectrum, indicating that the as-prepared CDs here were mainly composed of C (80.2 at.%), O (14.1 at.%) and N (5.7 at.%). The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows four peaks at 284.3, 285.5, 286.9 and 289.0 eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, CO and OCO, respectively. The O1s peaks located at 531.8 and 533.3 eV shown in Fig. 2d are associated with oxygen in the states of CO and COH/COC, respectively [23], which is consistent with the FTIR results. These results also indicate the good hydrophilicity of the as-prepared CDs due to the abundant hydroxyl and amino groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties, optical analysis and a proposed formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 3 a, a typical absorption peak at ca. 242 nm was observed, which is assigned to the π-π* transition of CC bond [24,25], another continuous absorbance broad band between 280 nm and 360 nm was also observed, which is attributed to n-π* transition resulting from CO along with a typical characteristic of fluorescent CDs [26,27]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs (Fig. 3b) showed the maximum emission at 420 nm and the maximum excitation at 360 nm. The full width at a half maximum (FWHM) at 420 nm was 100 nm. In addition, the CDs exhibit the excitation-dependent emission behavior. As they were excited at wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, the emission peak position shifted to longer wavelengths. The optimal emission peaks at 420 nm when excited at 360 nm and then the fluorescence intensity decreased remarkably. Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of the hollow carbon nanoparticles is measured to be 40.6% in aqueous solution using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QYR = 0.54) as the reference. The QY obtained through this preparation method is relatively much higher than many other reports [33–35].

Fig. 4 a shows the XRD patterns of pure PVA film and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film. The XRD pattern of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film show only one peak at 2θ = 19.4°, confirming the semicrystalline nature of this film, as was previously reported in the literature [28,29]. The XRD pattern of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film was similar with PVA film, which indicated that doped-Eu3+ and CDs were well exfoliated and uniformly dispersed in PVA matrix and the crystalline structure of PVA was slightly affected by the incorporation of doped-Eu3+ and CDs component. Fig. 4b gives the light transmittance spectra of PVA film and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film. At a visible wavelength between 390 and 780 nm, the light transmittance of PVA film and film keeps highly optically transparent (Inset in Fig. 4b). For PVA film, there is a weak absorption band at 278 nm, which is the character of PVA film [28,30]. Eu3+/CDs/PVA film shows a stronger absorbance in the UV region between 200 nm and 360 nm than that of pure PVA film. The stronger UV absorption may be ascribed to the CDs which also have a wide UV absorbance bands between 200 nm and 360 nm. In addition, a small absorbance band at 394 nm was observed in the light transmittance spectra of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film. The small absorbance band is ascribed to the electronic transitions of 7F0-5L6 of Eu3+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PL emission spectrum of the Eu3+/CDs/PVA thin film excited by different wavelength can be divided into two parts. One is the region between 350 nm and 550 nm. The peak in this region was originated from CDs in which the excitation-dependent PL behavior was kept. As shown in Fig. 5b, an obvious blue shift was observed between the CDs aqueous solution and Eu3+/CDs/PVA thin film's PL emission spectra, while there is a little different between the CDs aqueous solution's and Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution's PL emission spectra. The results indicated that the simple mixture of CDs aqueous and Eu3+/PVA solution will not cause the change of PL behavior of CDs, while the Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution becomes solidification state and leading to the formation of film, the PL property of CDs changed a lot. Although the excitation-dependent PL behavior was kept in Eu3+/CDs/PVA film, the blue shift between CDs aqueous solution and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film indicating the rigid and compact PVA matrix changed the energy between the first excited state and ground state of CDs.

The another region was between 570 nm and 640 nm (Fig. 5c). The narrow emission bands were due to the high non-radiative relaxations from the excited states of energy higher than 5D0 state. These are two obvious peaks at 590 nm and 615 nm which are assigned to the electronic transitions of 5D0-7F1 and 5D0-7F2 of Eu3+, respectively. The transitions of 5D0-7F1 with J = 1 has been identified as a magnetic dipole (MD) transition, which is a bright orange emission band. Another transition of from 5D0 to 7F2 was electric dipole (ED) transition, which is bright red emission. In particular, the transitions of 5D0-7F2 is considered to be hypersensitive transition that follows the selection rule of J = 2. Fluorescence intensity of the hypersensitive transition will significantly influenced by the host matrix environment around the RE3+ ions. The intensities of the electric dipole transitions could be changing based on the center of symmetry variations due to the changes in the host environment around the luminescent RE ion. Therefore, when doped-Eu3+ ions are situated at low symmetry sites, the ED transition has larger probability than the MD transition. By contrary, when doped-Eu3+ ions are situated at low symmetry sites, the MD transition has larger probability than the ED transition. The ratio between the emission intensities at 615 nm (5D0-7F2, ED) and 590 nm (5D0-7F1, MD) could bring out enough information to identify the host matrix environment around the Eu3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5c, the PL emission of both EuCl3 aqueous solution and Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution show a higher peak at 590 nm than the peak at 615 nm which indicated the host environment around Eu3+ in aqueous solution or CDs/PVA solution was fairly symmetrical. While the Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution becomes solidification and resulting in the formation of the film. The emission intensities at 615 nm become higher than that of at 590 nm, which indicated the host environment around Eu3+ in rigid and compact PVA matrix was fairly asymmetric. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetimes of the as-prepared CDs aqueous solution and as-prepared Eu3+/CDs/PVA film are 2.207 ns and 1.845 ns, respectively, which are shown in Fig. S1.

In addition, while the RE3+ gets into the polymer host, the RE3+ is expected to be coordinated with the polar groups in the host polymer matrix and complexation. The interaction will influence the local structure of the polymer backbone and affect certain infrared active modes of vibration. As shown in Fig. 5d, the ATR-IR spectra of pure PVA film and Eu3+/CDs/PVA film were compared. The infrared spectra are very similar. The broad band at 3000-3600 cm−1 due to the OH stretching vibration, the band at 2908 cm−1 assigned to the CH stretching vibration were observed and another absorption band at 1085 cm−1 was attributed to the hydroxy CO stretching band. Notably, compared with pure PVA, the absorption band at 3368 cm−1 became wide while the doped-Eu3+ and CDs were added to the PVA. The spectra indicated that the hydroxyl groups of PVA were interacting with the doped-Eu3+ and CDs. Moreover, it has been reported that absorption bands at 1141 cm−1 are sensitive to crystallinity [31]. The intensity of the absorption bands at 1141 cm−1 became weak while the doped-Eu3+ and CDs was added to PVA (Fig. 5e), which indicated the crystallinity of PVA become decreased after the Eu3+ and CDs were added. Further careful consideration of the spectrum shows that intensity of the absorption band at 1712 cm−1 slight decreased after the Eu3+ and CDs were added (Fig. 5f), in which the absorption band at 1712 cm−1 was ascribed to the residual acetate groups due to the manufacture of PVA from hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate [32]. The results indicated that the residual polyvinyl acetate in PVA also participate in the coordinate and form the complex. In order to make the results more convincing, some contrast experiments has been made as shown in Fig. S2 including the data for the film CDs/PVA without Eu3+ dopants, PVA alone, Eu3+/PVA.

A possible formation mechanism of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film was proposed based on the experimental results (Fig. 6 ). While the EuCl3 aqueous solution and CDs aqueous solution mix with PVA solution, the cation ion (Eu3+) and nanoparticles of CDs were still kept freedom of movement. The state of doped-Eu3+ and CDs in PVA solution was similar with them in aqueous solution and the host environment around CDs and doped-Eu3+ was fairly symmetrical. When the Eu3+/CDs/PVA solution become solidification, the PVA was crystallization. The surface polar groups of PVA interact with the hydroxyl groups of CDs and the doped-Eu3+ is expected to coordinate with the polar groups in the host polymer matrix and complex. As the PVA solidifies continue and form film finally, the PVA “caging” the CDs and doped-Eu3+ in isolation within the PVA-PVA hydrogen bonding matrix. The PVA-PVA hydrogen bonding matrix locks in the isolation of the doped-Eu3+ and CDs and hinders the doped-Eu3+and CDs motion offer a very rigid and compact in extremely stable films. The host environment around CDs and doped-Eu3+ become asymmetric. Due to the introduction of CDs and Eu3+, the crystallinity of PVA was decreased.

The concentration of Eu3+ dopants influences the PL intensity of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film by 340 nm excited as shown in Fig. 7 a. It can be seen that the relative intensity of the peaks centered at 590 nm and 615 nm became stronger with the higher concentration of Eu3+ dopants. Which mean that we can easily control the emission color of Eu3+/CDs/PVA by changing the concentration of Eu3+ dopants. Fig. 7b shows the (x, y) coordinates plotted in a CIE 1931 color diagram. When the concentration of doped-Eu3+ is tuned from 0.032 mmol to 0.72 mmol, the (x, y) coordinates the displacing from blue to purple. Thus, by adjusting the mixing proportion of CDs and doped-Eu3+, we can get a series of light conversion film.

Moreover, we designed experiments to measure the reusability and time stability of such films. The prepared Eu3+/CDs/PVA composite films with concentration of 1 mL (0.05 mol/L) of doped-Eu3+ solution, the fabricated film shows excellent stability, such that the intensity is still kept at 90.4% under continuous UV irradiation for 150 min as shown in Fig. S3a, compared with the most organic dyes of which the intensity is less than 80% after exposure for only 1 min [43], indicating its good stability under UV light. Meanwhile, we have also researched the stability of the film under daylight as shown in Fig. S3b, we collected and summarized the information of twenty days, every datum was received under the excitation wavelength (365 nm), then the integral areas of emission spectra (from 385 to 650 nm), were compared, as shown in Fig. S3b, is also exhibits excellent stability. Moreover, we measured the photoluminescence ability of the film for sixty times with recycling use, Fig. S3c indicates that the characteristic emission peaks of both CDs and doped-Eu3+ still keep outstanding and steady, nearly with no obvious shift or release of CDs and Eu3+. We can conclude that as-prepared film can keep outstanding reusability and stability, exhibiting its promising application prospect.

Blue- and red-light conversion film was prepared by embedding Eu3+ ions and carbon dots (CDs) in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solutions making the formation of Eu3+/CDs/PVA film, which exhibits excellent visible light-transmittance and UV absorption property. Our work successfully created an effective method for recombining of Eu3+ and CDs together, which stands out comparing to the previous complex preparation process. The fabricated Eu3+/CDs/PVA film is with tunable photoluminescence, which can serve for various plants growth. Meanwhile, this film can keep outstanding reusability and stability, exhibiting its promising application prospect.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 51203053, 51372091, 21571067), the Science and Technology Plan Projects of Guangdong Province (2014A010105035, 2015B090903074), the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2016.10.036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nucl. Instrum. Methods Phys. Res. B
J. Polym. Sci. Part A-1 Polym. Chem.
